items.prompt_md: In the 3Sum solution, why does the code skip an index `i` when `nums[i] == nums[i - 1]`?
options: To speed up the initial sort
To avoid re-fixing the same value and emitting the same triple twice
Because two equal values can never appear in a valid triple
items.answer: To avoid re-fixing the same value and emitting the same triple twice
items.explanation_md: Once a fixed value has been fully explored, re-fixing an equal neighboring value would just rediscover the same set of triples, so it's skipped to keep the results unique.

items.prompt_md: You must find every unique triple in a sorted (or sortable) array that sums to a target, and a triple-nested loop is too slow. Which approach fits?
options: For each index, run a two-pointer scan across the rest of the sorted array, skipping duplicate values
Hash every pairwise sum and look up the third value
Binary search for the third value for every possible pair
Sort and take every three adjacent elements
items.answer: For each index, run a two-pointer scan across the rest of the sorted array, skipping duplicate values
items.explanation_md: Fixing one index and reducing the remaining two slots to the sorted-array two-pointer scan is the k-sum move: it turns an O(n^3) triple loop into O(n^2).

items.prompt_md: What is the overall time complexity of the fix-one-then-two-pointer 3Sum solution?
items.explanation_md: The sort is O(n log n), and the outer loop over n fixed indices times an inner O(n) two-pointer scan gives O(n^2), which dominates the sort.
